business_info: Attacking work rate labels are historically derived from scout reports and may include legacy terms like 'high_press', 'medium_high', 'high_output', and others that were never fully standardized between seasons.

Internal analytics sometimes refer to a "proficiency composite" that blends vision, passing, and positioning, but in most day-to-day reporting individual skill ratings (e.g., long_passing, short_passing) are consumed directly without aggregating them.

The roster_tag field was added for contract workflow purposes only and was never backfilled for players who left before the tooling rollout; letters were chosen to be mnemonic in the original HR tool but those mnemonics are not captured in this database.

Analysts sometimes normalize long_passing using z-scores within each season when doing cross-era comparisons, but raw 0–100 ratings from Player_Attributes are preferred for simple leaderboards unless otherwise noted.

"Attacking effort" in scouting dossiers is usually discussed qualitatively (e.g., "relentless presser", "lazy off the ball") and only loosely mapped to the categorical attacking_work_rate values in the dataset, so direct comparisons between narrative reports and the numeric records should be treated with caution.

For passing-focused studies, some departments distinguish between "distribution index" (a custom blend of short_passing, long_passing, and vision) and raw attribute averages; legacy dashboards may still show the index even though newer work has moved back toward reporting skill ratings directly.
Analysts sometimes refer to long_passing above 80 as "elite range" when presenting to coaches, although this threshold is an internal convention and not encoded anywhere in the database schema.

Some scouting briefs group attacking_work_rate into broader buckets like low/standard/high, but the exact mapping from source strings (e.g., 'low', 'medium', 'high_press', 'high_output') to these buckets has varied by analyst and season.

The Player_Attributes table reflects video-game-style attribute ratings and should not be confused with internal physical tracking metrics; sprint_speed here is a subjective skill rating, not directly measured meters-per-second from GPS.

Roster planning tools sometimes label a player as "confirmed" or "provisional" for an upcoming season based on contract status, but those textual labels were collapsed into one-letter roster_tag codes during ETL, and the mapping file is maintained outside this dataset.

Confirmed roster standing in internal memos usually means the player is registered for league play and occupies a senior squad slot, as opposed to being on trial, in the academy, or co-owned; however, these nuances are not captured explicitly in the Match or Player_Attributes tables.

When reporting on "long passing" capabilities to non-technical staff, analysts sometimes mix quantitative long_passing ratings from the Player_Attributes table with qualitative comments about switch-of-play frequency from match footage, even though only the ratings are stored numerically here.

Some historical datasets experimented with a "technical index" but the european_football_2 schema deliberately keeps only atomic skill ratings (e.g., dribbling, ball_control, long_passing) so downstream teams can define their own indices per project.

In some internal performance reviews, staff will compute an ad hoc "proficiency" score as a simple average across several technical attributes for talking points, but there is no single standard formula enforced across all departments.

Analysts occasionally compute combined metrics like (vision + short_passing + long_passing) / 3 to summarize playmaking, but those experiments are usually stored in notebooks or slide decks rather than in the core database.

Terminology like "proficiency index", "distribution index", or "playmaking score" may appear interchangeably in historical decks depending on who authored them; always check the accompanying slide notes for the exact formula if you need to replicate those metrics.

The affiliated flag on the Player table was originally intended to distinguish players under direct club control from those tracked only for scouting reference, but not all vendors used the field consistently during data ingestion.

In some ETL jobs, free agents or out-of-contract players are temporarily given a neutral roster_tag value while paperwork is finalized, which means a player can appear in historic matches without ever having a clearly "confirmed" tag in the current snapshot.

Confirmed roster lists for competition registration are usually stored outside this database in league-operated systems; the Player.roster_tag column was meant only as a convenience snapshot for front-office tooling, not a fully authoritative source.

Seasonal analysis packs often focus on a moving two- or three-year window of Player_Attributes data, but the exact time span may change depending on whether the goal is contract planning, squad refresh, or historical benchmarking.

When analysts say "recent seasons" in slide decks, they might mean the last two completed campaigns, the last three calendar years, or simply the era after a major tactical shift; the term is intentionally loose unless a precise date filter is specified.

Some match reports will informally describe players as "top five long-range distributors" for a given league or season, but those rankings may mix subjective scouting impressions with underlying long_passing ratings and are not systematically recorded.

For simple communication, performance staff sometimes round long_passing ratings to the nearest 5 points when quoting to media or agents, even though internal queries and leaderboards should always operate on the full-precision stored values.
